discharge_readiness_rationale: Physician has marked discharge_ready: false due to two active teratogenic orders (lisinopril, atorvastatin) in a 7-week pregnant patient and no secured insulin supply in an uninsured T1DM patient with LACE 13 and four prior DKA admissions in 6 months — these constitute immediate and foreseeable patient safety hazards.

lace_score.implication: 30-day readmission risk approximately 33–40%. Per protocol, no discharge without bridge supports in place: insulin access secured, MFM referral with confirmed appointment, teratogenic medications discontinued and replaced, housing/food/transportation navigation actively in motion. Same-day coordination of home health is not clinically indicated (PT/functional status is intact), but pharmacy, social work, and specialty follow-up coordination is required before departure.

discharge_today_probability.interpretation: Discharge today is highly improbable. Three CRITICAL unresolved blockers combined with VERY_HIGH LACE tier and multiple HIGH unresolved items put this patient well below any safe discharge threshold until blockers are actively resolved.

No MFM referral placed or appointment confirmed for high-risk first-trimester pregnancy (HbA1c 11.4%)

prioritized_actions.action: Discontinue atorvastatin 20 mg from active orders; document postpartum reassessment plan

handoff_packages.pcp_summary:
Dear Colleague,

Sarah Williams is a 42-year-old woman with Type 1 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed age 14) and essential hypertension who was admitted via ED on 2026-04-18 in severe DKA (pH 7.14, bicarbonate 8, anion gap 28, HbA1c 11.4%, Kussmaul respirations, HR 128). On hospital Day 2, serum hCG of 28,450 mIU/mL confirmed a previously unrecognized intrauterine pregnancy at approximately 7 weeks gestation. DKA resolved over 5 days on IV insulin followed by transition to subcutaneous basal-bolus regimen; current ABG pH 7.39, bicarbonate 23, anion gap 12, glucose 112, urine ketones trace.

Discharge medications (reconciled): insulin glargine 22 units SC QHS (increased from home dose of 18 for pregnancy insulin resistance); insulin aspart per pregnancy-adjusted sliding scale AC meals + correction; prenatal vitamin daily; folic acid 4 mg daily (high-dose per ACOG for pregestational diabetes); pregnancy-safe antihypertensive (methyldopa, labetalol, or nifedipine ER — pending physician selection at discharge); nasal/injectable glucagon rescue. Lisinopril and atorvastatin have been discontinued as teratogens.

Active problems requiring follow-up: (1) T1DM with HbA1c 11.4% in first-trimester pregnancy — very high first-trimester malformation and loss risk; requires Maternal-Fetal Medicine as primary obstetric provider, not standard OB; (2) hypertension, transitioned off ACE inhibitor; (3) atorvastatin deferred to postpartum; (4) TSH 2.1 mIU/L (low-normal, recheck at first MFM visit); (5) obesity BMI 31.8.

LACE score 13 (VERY_HIGH) — 33–40% 30-day readmission risk driven by 4 prior DKA ED visits in 6 months attributable to systemic insurance and food access gaps rather than non-adherence. Patient is uninsured (Medicaid pending, pregnancy fast-track initiated), food-insecure, housing-unstable (6 weeks rent arrears), and lives alone. Insulin bridge supply secured at discharge via manufacturer assistance programs and hospital charity care; MFM appointment confirmed within 7 days; endocrinology follow-up within 2 weeks.

Recommended PCP follow-up within 1 week for care coordination, BP monitoring on new antihypertensive, and establishing continuity. Unresolved issues at discharge: emotional adjustment to unexpected pregnancy, long-term housing stability, and sustained medication access once Medicaid activates.

Thank you for your partnership in her care.

home_health_orders.visit_frequency: Home health not clinically indicated — patient is functionally independent (bicycle courier, no ADL deficits, no wound care, no IV access). If insurance-based home health DM education is available and acceptable to patient, recommend 2 visits in first 2 weeks focused on insulin regimen reinforcement and hypoglycemia/DKA monitoring. Otherwise defer to MFM/endocrinology outpatient follow-up.
home_health_orders.vital_monitoring: Patient self-monitoring: glucose fingerstick minimum fasting, pre-meal, bedtime (goal fasting <95, 1-hour postprandial <140, 2-hour <120 per pregnancy targets); BP daily (goal <140/90 on new antihypertensive); urine ketones if glucose >240 or during illness/nausea.

Glucose <70 mg/dL not responding to treatment within 15 minutes → treat again; if unresponsive after 2 attempts → 911
BP >160/110 or severe headache/visual changes → go to ED
Vomiting preventing oral intake or insulin >4–6 hours → ED (euglycemic DKA risk in pregnancy)
Any vaginal bleeding or cramping → call MFM same day
Rapid breathing, fruity breath, confusion, extreme fatigue → ED
medication_monitoring: Insulin glargine 22 units SC QHS — monitor for nocturnal hypoglycemia; dose expected to titrate upward through pregnancy
Insulin aspart per pregnancy sliding scale AC meals + correction — monitor pre/post-meal glucose; re-dose reconciliation at first MFM/endocrinology visit
New antihypertensive (methyldopa/labetalol/nifedipine ER) — monitor BP, orthostatic symptoms, fatigue
Folic acid 4 mg + prenatal vitamin — confirm adherence; document total daily folate
Glucagon rescue kit — verify patient and mother (remote) know how to use
home_health_orders.fall_precautions: Low fall risk at baseline. Hypoglycemia is the primary fall/safety risk, especially during cycling. Do not ride if glucose <90 mg/dL; carry fast-acting carbohydrate at all times during courier shifts.
home_health_orders.functional_status_at_discharge: Fully independent. All ADLs intact. Community ambulation unlimited. No assistive devices. Baseline activity: active bicycle courier.
goals_first_visit: Verify insulin supply in hand and correct injection technique
Reinforce pregnancy-adjusted sliding scale dosing and carbohydrate considerations
Teach-back DKA and hypoglycemia warning signs

DISCONTINUED: Atorvastatin 20 mg (teratogenic; deferred to postpartum)

Prenatal vitamin folate + 4 mg folic acid — intentional high-dose therapy for T1DM pregnancy per ACOG; confirm total daily folate content of specific prenatal vitamin dispensed and communicate to MFM

Dispensing logistics for manufacturer assistance programs — exactly how to refill

handoff_packages.patient_instructions:
You were in the hospital because your blood sugar got very high and made you very sick. We also found out you are about 7 weeks pregnant. Your blood sugar is better now.

Your medicines have changed:
- We stopped two old medicines (lisinopril and atorvastatin). They are not safe for the baby. Do NOT take them again, even if you find old pills at home.
- Your nighttime insulin dose is higher now: 22 units (it was 18 before).
- You have a new blood pressure pill. Take it every day.
- Take your prenatal vitamin AND the folic acid pill every day. You need both.
- You have a rescue medicine (glucagon) if your sugar gets too low and you can't fix it.

Call 911 or go to the ER right away if you have: fast breathing, fruity-smelling breath, throwing up more than a few hours, blood sugar over 300 with ketones, blood sugar under 70 that won't come up, bad headache with blurry vision, or any bleeding from the vagina.

Before you leave, make sure you have: your insulin, your appointment with the high-risk pregnancy doctor (MFM) within 1 week, and a phone number to call with questions. You are not alone in this.

meta.confidence_notes: All five agents provided detailed, internally consistent output. Critical flags (teratogenic medications, insulin access, MFM referral) are concordant across Physician, Nurse, Pharmacist, and MSW. PT appropriately defers on functional grounds. Gaps identified (glucagon prescription, IPV screen, sick-day rules, 24-hour contact) fall between individual agent scopes and are added by synthesis. The prenatal vitamin brand and exact folate content are unconfirmed in source data — pharmacist to reconcile at dispensing. Patient emotional state and IPV safety are data gaps, not negative findings.

functional_mobility.ambulation_distance: Unlimited community ambulation. Patient is a bicycle courier by occupation — functional endurance well above household and community thresholds.
functional_mobility.gait_quality: No gait deficits documented. Nursing and physician notes confirm independent, unrestricted mobility throughout hospitalization. No balance or coordination concerns noted.
functional_mobility.endurance: High baseline endurance consistent with active bicycle courier work. No deconditioning expected from 5-day hospitalization given full pre-admission activity level and rapid DKA resolution without prolonged bed rest.
functional_mobility.baseline_prior_admission: Fully active, unlimited community mobility. Bicycle courier — sustained aerobic physical activity as primary occupation and transportation.

contributing_factors: Hypoglycemia risk with new insulin regimen titrated upward for pregnancy — could precipitate acute functional impairment if glucose drops during activity
Newly confirmed pregnancy at 7 weeks — no balance or vestibular changes expected at this gestational age, but nausea/fatigue may emerge
Occupation-specific risk: bicycle courier work during pregnancy with hypoglycemia risk warrants separate safety discussion (outside PT scope but functionally relevant)
fall_risk.score: Low — per nursing fall risk documentation. No formal standardized tool score (e.g., Morse, STRATIFY) recorded in available data.
mitigation_recommendations: Ensure patient has glucose monitoring access and knows hypoglycemia protocol prior to return to active courier work
Educate on recognizing early hypoglycemia symptoms during physical exertion — this is the primary functional fall/safety risk given occupation
No home environmental fall mitigation required at this functional level
Reassess fall risk at OB/MFM and endocrinology follow-up visits as pregnancy progresses and center of gravity shifts

rehab_recommendation.justification: Patient is 42 years old with no functional deficits, no mobility impairment, no deconditioning, no musculoskeletal complaints, and a high-activity baseline as an active bicycle courier. DKA has resolved fully. She is fully independent in all ADLs and ambulation. There is no functional gap between current status and discharge environment (ground-floor studio apartment, no stairs, no structural access barriers). Physical therapy — in any form (IRF, SNF, home PT, outpatient PT, or structured HEP) — is not clinically indicated for this admission. The discharge barriers for this patient are pharmacologic, obstetric, and socioeconomic in nature, not functional or rehabilitative.

known_hazards: No structural hazards identified — ground-floor studio apartment with no stair negotiation required
Housing instability (month-to-month lease, 6 weeks behind on rent) represents a broader safety risk but not a physical environment hazard within PT scope
No area rugs, grab bar deficits, or access barriers documented — insufficient detail in record for full home safety assessment, but functional status does not suggest environmental modification need

insurance_access_conflict.detail: No PT services are clinically indicated; therefore no PT-specific insurance conflict exists. Note: the patient's uninsured status and pending Medicaid application are globally significant for medication and obstetric access — these conflicts are outside PT scope and are addressed by other team members.

flags.flag: Hypoglycemia risk during occupational physical exertion — bicycle courier returning to active work with upward-titrated insulin regimen and pregnancy
flags.action: Prior to discharge, patient requires explicit counseling (from endocrinology or diabetes education team) on hypoglycemia recognition, treatment, and safe glucose thresholds for sustained physical activity including cycling. This is a functional safety issue intersecting PT domain. PT defers clinical management to endocrinology but flags for team awareness.

flags.flag: No formal PT evaluation completed this admission — functional assessment based on nursing, physician mobility notes, and patient-reported occupational activity
flags.action: No PT evaluation is warranted given functional status; confidence in low-risk determination is high based on convergent data, but this is noted for documentation completeness.

flags.flag: As pregnancy progresses (second and third trimester), functional reassessment will be appropriate — pelvic girdle pain, postural changes, and balance shifts are common with T1DM pregnancy
flags.action: MFM or OB provider should include functional assessment referral trigger at approximately 20–24 weeks gestation, or sooner if patient reports musculoskeletal or balance complaints. Document in MFM referral communication.

pt.confidence_note: Confidence is high for the determination that PT services are not indicated and that the patient is functionally safe for home discharge. No formal PT evaluation was completed (none was clinically warranted), and the functional assessment is based on nursing ADL documentation, physician mobility notes, and the patient's verified occupational status as an active bicycle courier — all of which are internally consistent and point to intact, high-level functional status. The one area of functional concern (hypoglycemia during exertion) is pharmacologic in etiology and appropriately escalated to the endocrinology/diabetes education domain.

physician.discharge_hold_reason: CRITICAL: Two teratogenic medications (lisinopril, atorvastatin) remain on active orders and must be discontinued and, for lisinopril, replaced with a pregnancy-safe antihypertensive before discharge. Additionally, no insulin coverage is secured post-discharge in an uninsured patient who will develop DKA within days without insulin. These three issues constitute immediate patient safety hazards that must be resolved prior to discharge.
physician.clinical_summary: Sarah Williams is a 42-year-old woman with Type 1 diabetes mellitus and essential hypertension admitted via the ED on 2026-04-18 in severe DKA (pH 7.14, bicarbonate 8, anion gap 28, Kussmaul respirations, HR 128). She was discovered to be 7 weeks pregnant on hospital Day 2 via serum hCG — a pregnancy she was unaware of at admission. DKA has fully resolved over 5 days with IV insulin protocol followed by transition to subcutaneous basal-bolus regimen; current ABG and BMP are within normal limits, glucose 112, urine ketones trace, and vitals stable. However, two teratogenic home medications (lisinopril, atorvastatin) remain on active orders and have not been discontinued. HbA1c is 11.4%, placing this first-trimester pregnancy at very high risk for fetal malformation and obstetric complications; no MFM or OB care has been established. The patient is uninsured with no secured insulin supply for discharge.

acute_issues_resolved.evidence: ABG 2026-04-22: pH 7.39, bicarb 23, anion gap 12 (from pH 7.14/bicarb 8/AG 28 on admission). Urine ketones trace. Glucose 112 on subcutaneous insulin regimen. Vitals normalized (HR 80, RR 14). Clinically tolerating diet.
HR 80 on Day 5 (admission HR 128). BP 122/78. No vasopressor requirement. Fluid resuscitation complete.
Kussmaul respirations resolved. RR 14 currently. pCO2 38 mmHg (normalized from 22). Bicarb 23 mEq/L.
Glucose 112 on current SQ basal-bolus regimen. HbA1c 11.4% reflects chronic uncontrolled state — cannot resolve during this admission; requires outpatient MFM/endocrinology co-management.

acute_issues_resolved.issue: Unrecognized pregnancy — patient awareness and initial counseling
acute_issues_resolved.evidence: Patient informed Day 2. Social work consulted. Patient currently aware and alert/oriented x4. However, no OB/MFM established, no prenatal ultrasound completed, and teratogenic medications not yet discontinued — critical pregnancy-related issues remain unresolved.

pending_workup.impact_on_discharge: Not a hard discharge block, but given HbA1c 11.4% and first-trimester malformation risk, ultrasound should be completed in-house if feasible, or scheduled as urgent outpatient within 48 hours with confirmed appointment before discharge. Inability to confirm fetal viability and gestational dating prior to discharge is a moderate concern in this high-risk pregnancy.

contraindications.reason: ACE inhibitors are absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy. Second and third trimester exposure causes fetal renal tubular dysplasia, renal agenesis, oligohydramnios, skull hypoplasia, limb contractures, pulmonary hypoplasia, and neonatal death. First trimester exposure is associated with cardiovascular and CNS malformations. ACOG mandates immediate discontinuation upon pregnancy confirmation. Patient is 7 weeks gestation and the order remains active — this is an unresolved critical patient safety event.
contraindications.action_required: DISCONTINUE IMMEDIATELY before discharge. Physician must replace with a pregnancy-safe antihypertensive. Options include methyldopa, labetalol, or nifedipine (extended-release) — prescribing physician selects based on BP targets and patient factors. Do not discharge patient with lisinopril on active orders under any circumstances.

contraindications.reason: Statins are contraindicated throughout pregnancy. Atorvastatin carries historical FDA Category X designation for pregnancy. Statins inhibit cholesterol synthesis — a substrate essential for fetal steroid hormone production, neural development, and cell membrane formation. Risk of fetal harm outweighs any maternal lipid-lowering benefit. Active order has not been discontinued despite pregnancy confirmation 3 days ago.
contraindications.action_required: DISCONTINUE IMMEDIATELY before discharge. Hyperlipidemia management is deferred to the postpartum period. No alternative statin is safe in pregnancy. Physician must remove from active orders. Document intent to reassess and restart postpartum in discharge summary.

Provide Uber Health or Lyft Healthcare voucher if hospital program allows for first appointment. Map nearest T/bus stop to MFM clinic and provide written directions. (6) Confirm prenatal vitamins and folic acid 4mg filled at low/no cost before discharge. This is the most time-critical SDOH action in this case.
